note: Anti-indemnity statutes (e.g., Texas Civ. Prac. & Rem. Code § 151, California Civil Code § 2782) void or limit indemnities for a party's own negligence, especially in construction contracts.
The express negligence doctrine (notably in Texas) requires indemnification of a party's own negligence to be stated in conspicuous, specific language to be enforceable.
Courts generally enforce mutual indemnities the same as one-sided ones, provided the obligations are genuinely symmetric.
Whether 'defend' creates an immediate duty (upon tender) or only a reimbursement duty (after judgment) varies by state and by the clause's precise wording.
Exclusions typically carve out claims arising from the indemnitee's own gross negligence or willful misconduct, and often interact with the liability cap.
